sound_type: pik + soft rattle drum -- soft short pik call, gentle descending rattle, slower quieter drum. The quietest woodpecker in the Picidae streak. E=4.
ecosystem: Varied forest, suburban, riparian, orchards, parks, fragmented woodlots. The most flexible habitat requirements of any North American woodpecker.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Found across virtually all of North America.

coordinates.description: Reference for suburban/urban habitat. Downy Woodpeckers found in backyards, parks, and varied forest throughout the PNW.

taxonomy_note.description: Fifth consecutive Picidae (107-111). Dryobates pubescens -- North America's smallest woodpecker. Follows the Hairy Woodpecker (110) at consecutive degrees -- the great look-alike pair. Bill length is the key visual field mark. Sound quality is the key auditory mark (pik vs. peek!). E=4 -- energy descent continues from Pileated E=6 (108-109) through Hairy E=5 (110) to Downy E=4 (111).
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 8 entries (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn Woodpecker 107, Pileated Woodpecker laughing 108, Pileated Woodpecker drumming 109, Hairy Woodpecker 110, Downy Woodpecker 111)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Dryobates from Greek drys ('tree') + bates ('one who treads') -- 'tree-treader.' pubescens from Latin pubescens ('downy, soft-haired') -- referring to the soft white feathers on the back. The Downy Woodpecker: the soft-feathered tree-treader.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Dryobates -- formerly placed in Picoides. Reclassified following molecular phylogenetic studies. The Downy Woodpecker is NOT closely related to the Hairy Woodpecker despite visual similarity -- the resemblance is likely Mullerian mimicry.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Piciformes. Fifth consecutive (107-111). After the Corvidae septet (Passeriformes 100-106), five consecutive Picidae (Piciformes 107-111).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae continues for a fifth consecutive degree. Approaching the Corvidae septet's record of seven consecutive.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 3,000-8,000 Hz. Higher-pitched than the Hairy Woodpecker (2,000-7,000 Hz). The pik call is a short, soft note in the 3-8 kHz range.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 4,000-6,000 Hz. Higher peak energy than the Hairy, contributing to the softer, thinner quality.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: pik call: 3,000-8,000 Hz. Rattle: similar range, rapid series of pik notes descending. Drum: low-frequency mechanical percussive.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: pik call: ~0.05 seconds (very short). Rattle: 1-2 seconds. Drum: 1-2 seconds.
song_metrics.note_structure: PIK: Single short downslurred note. Softer and shorter than the Hairy's peek!. RATTLE: Rapid series of pik notes descending in pitch, running together into a continuous gentle rattle.
song_metrics.note_types: pik call (primary contact/alarm), descending rattle (territorial/contact), drum (territorial/mate attraction). The pik call is the diagnostic vocalization.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single notes (pik) or descending series (rattle). Simple. The rattle is the most complex vocalization -- a rapid series that descends.
song_metrics.timbre: Soft. Thin. Gentle. The pik has a papery quality -- light and unassuming. The rattle is rolling and descending. The drum is quiet and rapid. Nothing about the Downy's acoustic profile is emphatic.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- MODERATE energy. The softest woodpecker in the Picidae streak (107-111). The pik is easy to overlook. The drum is quiet. Everything about the Downy is understated compared to the Hairy (E=5) and the Pileated (E=6).
song_metrics.tempo: The rattle is rapid -- individual notes run together. The drum is approximately 15-25 strikes per second (slower than the Hairy's 25-30).
song_metrics.context: CONTACT AND TERRITORIAL. The pik call maintains contact between pair members and flock associates. The rattle is territorial. The drum is territorial and mate-attraction. The Downy's calls are social -- it is often part of mixed-species flocks.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. The pik call and rattle are consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: SOFT PIK + GENTLE RATTLE -- the quietest woodpecker. E=4. North America's smallest woodpecker. Follows the Hairy Woodpecker (110) at consecutive degrees -- the great look-alike pair. Fifth consecutive Picidae (107-111). Energy descent: E=6 (108-109), E=5 (110), E=4 (111). Suburban specialist. Most successfully urbanized woodpecker.

plumage.overall: Small woodpecker -- sparrow-sized. Black and white. Black upperparts with white back (central white stripe). Black wings with white spots/bars. White underparts. Black tail with white outer tail feathers barred with black. Males have small red patch on nape.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 111: (1) SMALL SIZE -- sparrow-sized, clearly smaller than Hairy. (2) SHORT BILL -- proportionally about half head width, distinctly shorter than Hairy's. (3) BARRED OUTER TAIL FEATHERS -- white outer tail feathers with black bars (Hairy's are unbarred white). (4) SOFT PIK CALL -- distinctly softer than Hairy's loud peek!.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Males have red nape patch; females lack it.
plumage.flight_pattern: Undulating. Typical woodpecker flight. Small size conspicuous -- noticeably smaller than the Hairy Woodpecker in flight.

physical_description.overall: Small woodpecker. Body length 14-18 cm. Wingspan 25-30 cm. Weight 21-28 g. North America's smallest woodpecker. Black and white plumage. Short stubby bill. Males with small red nape patch.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 111: sparrow-sized black-and-white woodpecker with short stubby bill, giving soft pik calls. In any habitat with trees -- including suburban backyards. Bill length relative to head is the key field mark separating from the Hairy.
physical_description.field_id: Very small black-and-white woodpecker with short bill. Soft pik call. Found in varied habitats including suburbs. If it looks like a miniature Hairy Woodpecker with a tiny bill -- it's a Downy.

nodes.title: Downy vs. Hairy -- The Great Look-Alike Pair
nodes.description: Consecutive degrees (110-111). Visually similar, acoustically different. Hairy: loud peek!, E=5, long bill, mature forest. Downy: soft pik, E=4, short bill, varied habitat. The most important look-alike pair in North American birding. Bill length is the key visual mark. Sound quality is the key auditory mark.

nodes.title: Soft pik + Gentle Rattle -- The Quiet Woodpecker
nodes.description: E=4. Soft short pik call. Gentle descending rattle. Slower quieter drum. The quietest woodpecker in the Picidae streak. Energy descent: E=6 (108-109), E=5 (110), E=4 (111).

nodes.title: North America's Smallest Woodpecker -- Size as Superpower
nodes.description: 14-18 cm, 21-28 g. Smallest woodpecker on the continent. Small size enables foraging on thin branches, weed stems, and in suburban habitats unavailable to larger woodpeckers. The smallest is also the most adaptable.

nodes.title: Suburban Specialist -- Ecological Flexibility
nodes.description: Most successfully urbanized woodpecker. Thrives in backyards, parks, orchards, fragmented woodlots. Comes to suet feeders. Population stable to increasing. A model of ecological adaptability.

nodes.title: Nuclear Species in Mixed-Species Flocks
nodes.description: In fall and winter, Downy Woodpeckers join mixed-species flocks with chickadees, nuthatches, creepers, and kinglets. The Downy serves as a nuclear species -- its presence attracts and stabilizes the flock. Social role disproportionate to body size.

nodes.title: Fifth Consecutive Picidae (107-111)
nodes.description: Acorn (107), Pileated laughing (108), Pileated drumming (109), Hairy (110), Downy (111). Five consecutive Picidae. Approaching the Corvidae septet's record of seven.

cross_references.relationship: THE GREAT LOOK-ALIKE PAIR. Consecutive degrees (110-111). Visually similar, acoustically different. Hairy: loud peek!, E=5, long bill, mature forest. Downy: soft pik, E=4, short bill, varied habitat. Bill length is the key.
PICIDAE SIZE CONTRAST. Pileated is the largest (40-49 cm, E=6). Downy is the smallest (14-18 cm, E=4). Same family, maximum size range. Three degrees apart.
PICIDAE ENERGY DESCENT. Pileated drumming E=6 (109), Hairy E=5 (110), Downy E=4 (111). Three-degree descent from maximum to moderate within Picidae.
PICIDAE STREAK. Fifth consecutive (107-111). Acorn started the streak. Downy continues it. Oak savanna (107) to suburban backyards (111).
PICIDAE FAMILY. Engine's first woodpecker (21-22). Both common, widespread, familiar. The woodpeckers most people encounter first.
